payload.text:
ضيف هي الاسئلة


Simple Squamous Epithelium




Major Functions: Diffusion and filtration


Locations: Kidneys - filtration membrane


lungs - alveolar (air sac) walls


Capillary walls




Endothelium - slick, friction reducing layer of simple squamous epithelium that lines the lymphatic vessels, blood vessels, and the heart.




Mesothelium - the epithelium of serous membranes in the ventral body cavity


Simple Cuboidal Epithelium




Major Functions: secretion and absorption


Location: Smallest ducts of glands and many kidney tubules

Cancellous (Spongy) Bone Tissue


- Bony plates called trabeculae. Lighter than compact bone, but still strong


- Locations - in ends of long bones and inside all other bones

Reticular Connective Tissue


- Loose connective tissue


- Resembles areolar but fibers are reticular fibers


- Provides a framework for organs


- Found in lymph nodes, the spleen, the liver, and bone marrow

Adipose Tissue


- Lose connective tissue


Functions: insulation, energy storage, and shock absorber


- Similar to areolar in structure but greater nutrient storage


- Adipocytes are filled with triglycerides (lipids)

Nervous Tissue


- Main component of nervous system


Location: Brain, spinal cord, nerves


Functions: regulates and controls body functions




2 Major Cell Types:


Neurons - specialized nerve cells that generate and conduct nerve impulses


Neuroglia - Supporting cells that support, insulate, and protect neurons

Hyaline Cartilage


- Flexible, yet rigid. Appears glassy


- Lots of collagen fibers in the matrix, not visible because the optical density is the same as the matrix


Location: ends of long bones, tracheal rings, larynx, costal (rib) cartilages, nose, most of the embryonic skeleton

Transitional Epithelium


- 3 to 6 layers of cells that have the ability to change shape


- Cells appear cube shaped or columnar with domed shaped apical surfaces


- When distended (bloated), cells appear flattened




Major Function: Stretch


Location: Urinary bladder, ureters, part of the urethra. Allows more urine to be stored in the urinary bladder

Elastic Cartilage


- Lots of elastin fibers. Strength and ability to stretch.


Location: external ear and epiglottis (thing that hangs in back of throat)

Blood


- Is a fluid (Most atypical CT. No support or binding)


- Consists of blood cells surrounded by a nonliving fluid matrix - Plasma


- Red blood cells are most common cell type


- Also contains white blood cells and platelets (cell fragments - pinched off portions of cell, initiate clot formation)


Functions: in transport - O2, CO2, nutrients, wastes, hormones, etc...)

Cardiac Muscle


- Found in the walls of heart, involuntary, one nucleus per cell, branching cells, striated, intercalated discs

Smooth Muscle


- Nonstriated, involuntary, one nucleus per cell, tapered ends of cells


- Location: mainly in walls of hollow organs such as the stomach and intestine

Areolar Connective Tissue


Loose connective tissue


Functions: Supports and binds other tissues. The most widely distributed connective tissue

Stratified Squamous Epithelium


- Most widespread of stratified epithelia


- Free surface squamous; deeper layers cuboidal or columnar


- Located in areas subject to wear and tear


- Cells farthest from basal layer (and nutrients) are less viable




Major Functions: Protect


Location: External part of the skin and extends a short distance into every body opening that is directly continuous with the skin.

Dense Regular Connective Tissue


- Closely packed bundles of collagen fibers running parallel to the direction of pull


- Location: tendons and ligaments

Simple Columnar Epithelium




Major Functions: absorption and secretion


Location: Lines the digestive tract from the stomach through the rectum


- May have microvilli, cilia, goblet cells, or tubular glands

Elastic Connective Tissue


Dense connective tissue


- Contains a high proportion of elastic fibers. Allows tissues to recoil after stretching


- Location: walls of large arteries and bronchial tubes, certain ligaments

Skeletal Muscle


- Contractible, little to no matrix, highly vascularized


- Attached to bones of the skeleton, voluntary, striated. Multinucleate cells are called muscle fibers

Pseudostratified Columnar Epithelium


- One layer but looks like many layers of columnar cells


- Cells vary in height with cell nuclei at different levels


- All cells reach the basement membrane, but not all reach the free surface


- May have cilia and goblet cells




Major Functions: Secretion and absorption


Location: Upper respiratory tract, male's sperm carrying ducts

Compact Bone Tissue


Also called osseous tissue. Very hard. Solid matrix.


- Supports and protects body structures


- Cavities in bone are the site of blood production and storage sites for fat.


- Osteocytes are located in lacunae


- Good blood supply




- looks solid to the naked eye


Location: in shafts of long bones and outside of all bones

Dense Irregular Connective Tissue


- Closely packed bundles of collagen fibers, but bundles are thicker and irregularly shaped


- Location: in the dermis of the skin, sclera aka part of eye, joint capsules, and coverings around some organs

Fibrocartilage


- Alternating rows of chondrocytes and rows of collagen fibers


- Compressible and resists tension


- Location - intervertebral discs, pubic symphysis, menisci of knees

Stratified cuboidal and stratified columnar

Are present in the body but rare

**Examining Table Structure**

I'm currently focused on the user's request to incorporate questions into the app. I've successfully identified the HTML table with the ID "flashCardsListingTable" containing the flashcard data, and am now analyzing the structure of the rows. I see each `<tr>` element holds both the question and answer within separate table cells.

I'm now diving into the specifics of the data within each table cell. The question cell can have an image or text, or both! I'm planning to extract the image source and text separately, then use a fallback question text if the front text is empty, defaulting to something like, "Identify this". The answer cell only has text. Based on earlier examples, the category seems to be "Chapter 4: Tissues." I will parse the data into the appropriate `Question` format used in `data.ts`, extracting the various fields. I will consider splitting the definitions from the `answerText` and putting them into an `explanation` field.

The answer often appears as the first line of the back of the card and the detail in the rest of the text.  I will try to separate the `answerText` and `explanation` by taking the first line of the answer text as the main answer, and the rest as context. diffs.replacement:
import { Question } from './types';

export const questions: Question[] = [
  // --- Chapter 7: Axial Skeleton ---
  {
    id: "q-71",
    questionText: "Which bone of the Axial Skeleton articulates with every other cranial bone and is often referred to as the keystone of the cranial floor?",
    answerText: "Sphenoid bone",
    category: "Chapter 7: Axial Skeleton",
    explanation: "The sphenoid bone sits in the middle of the skull base and contacts all other cranial bones, holding them together.",
    options: ["Ethmoid bone", "Occipital bone", "Temporal bone", "Sphenoid bone"]
  },
  {
    id: "q-72",
    questionText: "The Atlas (C1 vertebra) is distinct from other vertebrae because it:",
    answerText: "Lacks a vertebral body and a spinous process",
    category: "Chapter 7: Axial Skeleton",
    explanation: "The atlas is a ring of bone consisting of anterior and posterior arches and large lateral masses.",
    options: ["Has the largest vertebral body", "Lacks a vertebral arch", "Lacks a vertebral body and a spinous process", "Has a prominent dens (odontoid process)"]
  },
  {
    id: "q-121",
    questionText: "The Atlanto-Axial Joint (between C1 and C2) is functionally classified as a Diarthrosis and structurally as a:",
    answerText: "Pivot joint",
    category: "Chapter 7: Axial Skeleton",
    explanation: "This joint allows rotation of the head (shaking head \"no\") around the dens of the axis.",
    options: ["Hinge joint", "Pivot joint", "Condyloid joint", "Saddle joint"]
  },
  {
    id: "q-122",
    questionText: "Which bone is considered the keystone of the facial skeleton because it articulates with almost all other facial bones?",
    answerText: "Maxilla",
    category: "Chapter 7: Axial Skeleton",
    explanation: "The maxillae form the upper jaw and articulate with every facial bone except the mandible.",
    options: ["Mandible", "Zygomatic", "Maxilla", "Nasal"]
  },
  {
    id: "q-110",
    questionText: "The Zygomatic bone is known as the:",
    answerText: "Cheekbone",
    category: "Chapter 7: Axial Skeleton",
    explanation: "The zygomatic bones form the prominences of the cheeks and part of the lateral wall and floor of each orbit.",
    options: ["Forehead bone", "Cheekbone", "Lower jaw bone", "Upper jaw bone"]
  },
  {
    id: "q-125",
    questionText: "The Fontanels (soft spots) in the infant skull are composed of:",
    answerText: "Fibrous connective tissue",

    questionText: "Explain why the shoulder joint is both more mobile and weaker than the hip joint.",
    answerText: "The shoulder joint has a shallow socket (the glenoid fossa) into which the head of the humerus fits, and the labrum only slightly deepens the socket. The head of the humerus is larger in relation to the socket. It is deepened by a number of ligaments, but because of its structure, tends to be weaker than the hip joint. It is highly movable and can rotate easily. The hip joint has a deeper socket (acetabulum) into which the head of the femur inserts and is also strengthened by ligaments.     id: "q-joint-43",
    questionText: "Explain why the shoulder joint is both more mobile and weaker than the hip joint.",
    answerText: "The shoulder joint has a shallow socket (the glenoid fossa) into which the head of the humerus fits, and the labrum only slightly deepens the socket. The head of the humerus is larger in relation to the socket. It is deepened by a number of ligaments, but because of its structure, tends to be weaker than the hip joint. It is highly movable and can rotate easily. The hip joint has a deeper socket (acetabulum) into which the head of the femur inserts and is also strengthened by ligaments. Because of the angle at which the femur fits into its socket, along with the depth of the socket, the hip is a stronger, less mobile joint, made to support the weight of the body.",

payload.text:
ضيف هي الاسئلة
Terms in this set (85)
Epithelial, Connective, Muscle, Nervous
Which tissue is found in the stomach?
Adherens junctions and desmosomes
These types of cell junctions anchor adjacent cells together and resist their separation during contractile activities.
B, D, E
Which statements describe the functions of the basement membrane? Select all that apply.
a) The basal lamina is a specialized type of extracellular material only secreted by connective tissue cells.b) Blood vessels in connective tissue do not penetrate the basement membrane, requiring nutrients to diffuse to the epithelium tissue.
c) Hemidesmosomes connect the reticular lamina to the connective tissue.d) Epithelial cells produce the laminin which attach to the integrins in hemidesmosomes. e) Basement membrane helps support and guide cells in migration during tissue repair.
1. Epithelial tissue 2. Desmosome junctions 3. Gap junctions 4. Apical 5. basal 6. hemidesomome junctions
Fill in the blank: (1) covers and protects surfaces inside and outside of the body. (2) help secure most of the epidermal cells together through intermediate filaments, while (3) allow communication among the epidermal cells. This tissue will have a(n) (4) surface that is at the superficial layer, and a(n) (5) surface that is attached to the basement membrane by (6)
Based on cell shape, epithelial tissues can be divided into four categories including: 1) squamous which are thin and flat, 2) cuboidal which are similar in width and height, 3) columnar are taller than they are wide and 4) transitional which can change shape from cuboidal to squamous and back again. Epithelial tissues can also be categorized based on the number of layers of cells found in the tissue. These categories include: 1) simple which means a single layer of cells, 2) stratified which is two or more layers of cells and 3) pseudostratified which is one layer of cells that appears multi-layered due to the arrangement of the nuclei in the cells.
Name and briefly describe the different categories of epithelial tissues based on cell shape and number of layers of cells found in the tissue.
Simple squamous epithelium
Which epithelial tissue is found lining the kidney glomerulus, and performs blood filtration?
Keratinized stratified squamous epithelium
Which epithelial tissue forms the most superficial layer of the skin
Stratified cuboidal epithelium
Which epithelial tissue lines the ducts of sweat glands and oil glands?
Ciliated simple columnar epithelium
Which epithelial tissue lines most bronchioles and fallopian tubes and moves mucus and oocytes?
Lining of the urinary bladder
In which area would transitional epithelial cells be found?
Multicellular exocrine
[________] glands are categorized by whether their ducts are branched or
simple branched acinar gland; holocrine
Sebaceous glands are classified as ________ and are functionally classified as ________
how the gland releases its secretory product
Functional classification of exocrine glands is based on..
holocrine
Due to the entire cell being secreted, [ ________ ] glands will produce the most viscous secretion.
protein fibers and ground substance
The extracellular matrix of connective tissue consists of ...
keratinocytes
Mesenchyme will differentiate into all EXCEPT...
a) osteoblasts.b) Erythrocytes. c) chondroblasts. d) fibroblasts.e) keratinocytes.
ground substance
The ________ component of connective tissue is found between the cells and fibers, and functions to support and bind cells in the tissue together.
Hyaluronic acid
Which polysaccharide is commonly found in the ground substance of connective tissues?
1. reticular 2. collagen 3. elastic
Match the appropriate fiber with its function.
1. modified collagen fibers used for reinforcement
2. strongest and most abundant protein 3.allow extensibility, usually without breaking
stroma
The ________ is formed from reticular fibers that support soft organs such as the spleen and lymph nodes.
1. reticular connective tissue 2. elastic connective tissue 3. cartilage 4. lymph
Match the type of tissue to its classification
1. Loose connective tissue:
2. Dense connective tissue:
3. Supporting Connective tissue:
4. Liquid connective tissue:
a, b, e
Which is a location where adipose tissue is commonly found? Select all that apply.
a) Subcutaneous layer deep to the skin b) Lining the chambers of the heartc) Surrounding the braind) Inside the epidermis
e) In the articular cartilage of joints
hyaline cartilage and areolar connective tissue
Anne suffers from Ehlers-Danlos, a rare inherited disorder of connective tissue, specifically affecting collagen fibers. Because many connective tissues contain collagen throughout the body, she has issues with many tissues, joints, and skin. The ligaments and tendons are loose and heal poorly when stretched too much. Her kneecap pops out of the groove a lot. What specific connective tissue is being affected? Select all that apply.
fibrocartilage
The pubic symphysis and intervertebral discs are composed of
Growth of cartilage can be classified as interstitial or appositional. In interstitial growth, the cartilage increases rapidly in size due to the division of existing chondrocytes and the continuous deposition of increasing amounts of matrix by the chondrocytes. In appositional growth, activity of the cells in the inner chondrogenic layer of the perichondrium leads to growth. Deeper layers divide and cells mature resulting in the matrix accumulating beneath the perichondrium on the outer surface of the cartilage causing it to grow in width.
Name and briefly describe the two types of growth seen in cartilage.
Osteon
What is the basic structural unit of compact bone tissue?
haversian canals.
Spongy bone tissue lacks
1. plasma 2. chondroitin sulfate 3. collagen
Match the matrix with the correct connective tissue.
1. Blood
2 Cartilage
3. Dense regular
Synovial membrane
Which membrane lines freely moveable joints?
Serous membrane
Which membrane reduces friction between organs?
stem cells
Immature, undifferentiated cells that can divide to replace lost or damaged cells are called
C

Each osteon includes lamellae, lacunae, canaliculi and a Haversian canal. The lamellae are concentric rings of matrix that consist of mineral salts and collagen fibers. Lacunae are small spaces between lamellae that contain mature bone cells called osteocytes. Projecting from the lacunae are canaliculi, which are networks of minute canals containing the processes of osteocytes. Canaliculi provide routes for nutrients to reach osteocytes and for wastes to leave them. A central Haversian canal contains blood vessels and nerves. Trabeculae of spongy bone have similar components but lack the haversian system. Trabeculae still contain lamellae osteocytes, lacunae, and canaliculi. The spaces within the trabeculae are filled with red bone marrow.
Briefly describe the four parts of an osteon and compare it to trabeculae.
Connective tissue can contain fibroblasts, which are large, flat cells with branching projections that secrete the fibers and ground substance of the matrix. Macrophages develop from monocytes and are irregular shaped with short branching projections and are capable of engulfing bacteria and cellular debris by phagocytosis. Plasma cells secrete antibodies that attack or neutralize foreign substances in the body. Mast cells produce histamine that dilates small blood vessels. Adipocytes store triglycerides. White blood cells migrate from blood to connective tissue in response to certain immune system conditions.
Name and describe the types of cells commonly found in connective tissue.
A and C
In the light micrograph, which two tissues contain gap junctions?
smooth muscle
[ ________ ] tissue moves food through the digestive tract?
Cardiac muscle, smooth muscle
________ muscle pumps blood through the body and ________ is located in the wall of blood vessels?
Both cardiac and skeletal muscle tissues are striated and smooth muscle is not, hence its name. Skeletal muscle cells have more than one nucleus and are large and run parallel to each other. Cardiac muscle cells usually have only one nucleus and are branched. They also are connected to each other via gap junctions and desmosomes. This area is known as the intercalated discs. Smooth muscle cells are small spindle-shaped cells that form thick layers around the hollow organs. Like cardiac muscle cells, some smooth muscle cells communicate via gap junctions. Skeletal muscle is voluntarily controlled while both cardiac and smooth muscle tissue are involuntary.
Compare and contrast the functional and structural characteristics of the three types of muscle tissue.
Muscle cells and neurons

In order to replace the worn-out, damaged or dead cells, new cells arise from stroma, supporting connective tissue, or the parenchyma for replacement. In some cases stem cells, which are the immature and undifferentiated cells, will divide to replace the lost or damaged cells.
Discuss how a damaged tissue is repaired.
The secretory product (hormones) of endocrine glands enters the interstitial fluid and then diffuses into the blood without going through a duct. Exocrine glands secrete their products into ducts that empty onto the surface of a covering or the lumen of a hollow organ. Sweat, oil, earwax, saliva and digestive enzymes are typical exocrine gland secretions.
Discuss the difference(s) between an endocrine and an exocrine gland.

Electrical signals called ________ travel along the plasma membrane of a neuron and stimulate the release of chemicals called ________ that are then used to communicate with other cells.
The classification is based on how their secretion is released. Merocrine gland secretions are packaged into a secretary vesicle and released via exocytosis. Apocrine glands accumulate their secretory product at the apical surface of the cell and the upper portion of the cell pinches off from the rest of the cell to release the products. Holocrine glands accumulate their product in their cytosol and after the cell matures, ruptures and releases the product.
Discuss the three functional classifications of exocrine glands secretions.
A, D, E
Anne suffers from Ehlers-Danlos, a rare inherited disorder of connective tissue, specifically affecting collagen fibers. b) Elastic connective tissue

d) Areolar connective tissue

Areolar connective tissue in the papillary region of the dermis and the subcutaneous region of the body. Reticular connective tissue's reticular fibers would be affected due to them being modified collagen. The stroma would not be as supportive. Dense regular in ligaments and tendons. Dermis, sclera and the periosteum and perichondrium's dense irregular would be affected. Elastic connective tissue located in lungs and blood vessels. Fibrocartilage located in the meniscus and intervertebral discs. Bone would lose much of its strength.

Select all that apply.", answerText: "hyaline cartilage and areolar connective tissue", category: "Chapter 4: Tissue Level", options: ["dense regular and dense irregular", "hyaline cartilage and areolar connective tissue", "elastic cartilage and adipose", "bone and blood"] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-24", questionText: "The pubic symphysis and intervertebral discs are composed of", answerText: "fibrocartilage", category: "Chapter 4: Tissue Level", options: ["hyaline cartilage", "elastic cartilage", "fibrocartilage", "dense regular connective tissue"] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-25", questionText: "Name and briefly describe the two types of growth seen in cartilage.", answerText: "Growth of cartilage can be classified as interstitial or appositional. In interstitial growth, the cartilage increases rapidly in size due to the division of existing chondrocytes and the continuous deposition of increasing amounts of matrix by the chondrocytes. In appositional growth, activity of the cells in the inner chondrogenic layer of the perichondrium leads to growth. Deeper layers divide and cells mature resulting in the matrix accumulating beneath the perichondrium on the outer surface of the cartilage causing it to grow in width.", category: "Chapter 4: Tissue Level", options: [] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-26", questionText: "What is the basic structural unit of compact bone tissue?", answerText: "Osteon", category: "Chapter 4: Tissue Level", options: ["Trabeculae", "Osteon", "Lamellae", "Lacunae"] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-27", questionText: "Spongy bone tissue lacks", answerText: "haversian canals.", category: "Chapter 4: Tissue Level", options: ["osteocytes.", "haversian canals.", "lamellae.", "lacunae."] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-28", questionText: "Match the matrix with the correct connective tissue.

"Chapter 4: Tissue Level", options: ["A", "B", "C", "None"] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-48", questionText: "Briefly describe the four parts of an osteon and compare it to trabeculae.", answerText: "Each osteon includes lamellae, lacunae, canaliculi and a Haversian canal. The lamellae are concentric rings of matrix that consist of mineral salts and collagen fibers. Lacunae are small spaces between lamellae that contain mature bone cells called osteocytes. Projecting from the lacunae are canaliculi, which are networks of minute canals containing the processes of osteocytes. Canaliculi provide routes for nutrients to reach osteocytes and for wastes to leave them. A central Haversian canal contains blood vessels and nerves. Trabeculae of spongy bone have similar components but lack the haversian system. Trabeculae still contain lamellae osteocytes, lacunae, and canaliculi. The spaces within the trabeculae are filled with red bone marrow.", category: "Chapter 4: Tissue Level", options: [] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-49", questionText: "Name and describe the types of cells commonly found in connective tissue.", answerText: "Connective tissue can contain fibroblasts, which are large, flat cells with branching projections that secrete the fibers and ground substance of the matrix. Macrophages develop from monocytes and are irregular shaped with short branching projections and are capable of engulfing bacteria and cellular debris by phagocytosis. Plasma cells secrete antibodies that attack or neutralize foreign substances in the body. Mast cells produce histamine that dilates small blood vessels. Adipocytes store triglycerides. White blood cells migrate from blood to connective tissue in response to certain immune system conditions.", category: "Chapter 4: Tissue Level", options: [] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-50", questionText: "In the light micrograph, which two tissues contain gap junctions?", answerText: "A and C", questionImage: "https://o.quizlet.com/m0nvlRYRm6VlbJDrfbcaqg.png", category: "Chapter 4: Tissue Level", options: ["A and B", "B and C", "A and C", "None"] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-51", questionText: "[ ________ ] tissue moves food through the digestive tract?", answerText: "smooth muscle", category: "Chapter 4: Tissue Level", options: ["skeletal muscle", "cardiac muscle", "smooth muscle", "epithelial"] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-52", questionText: "________ muscle pumps blood through the body and ________ is located in the wall of blood vessels?", answerText: "Cardiac muscle, smooth muscle", category: "Chapter 4: Tissue Level", options: ["Skeletal, smooth", "Cardiac, skeletal", "Cardiac, smooth", "Smooth, cardiac"] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-53", questionText: "Compare and contrast the functional and structural characteristics of the three types of muscle tissue.", answerText: "Both cardiac and skeletal muscle tissues are striated and smooth muscle is not, hence its name. Skeletal muscle cells have more than one nucleus and are large and run parallel to each other. Cardiac muscle cells usually have only one nucleus and are branched. They also are connected to each other via gap junctions and desmosomes. This area is known as the intercalated discs. Smooth muscle cells are small spindle-shaped cells that form thick layers around the hollow organs. Like cardiac muscle cells, some smooth muscle cells communicate via gap junctions. Skeletal muscle is voluntarily controlled while both cardiac and smooth muscle tissue are involuntary.", category: "Chapter 4: Tissue Level", options: [] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-54", questionText: "Which cells are considered excitable cells because they are able to produce electrical signals?", answerText: "Muscle cells and neurons", category: "Chapter 4: Tissue Level", options: ["Epithelial and muscle", "Connective and nervous", "Muscle cells and neurons", "Adipocytes and neurons"] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-55", questionText: "Which of the light micrographs in the figure below shows the type of epithelial tissue whose functions include filtration, diffusion, osmosis, and secretion in serous membranes?", answerText: "A", questionImage: "https://o.quizlet.com/Jt0PViHO8nwyuQTb03-saw.png", category: "Chapter 4: Tissue Level", options: ["A", "B", "C", "D"] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-56", questionText: "Which of the light micrographs shows epithelial tissue that forms thyroid gland and secretes hormones?", answerText: "B", questionImage: "https://o.quizlet.com/DiEgF-fx9Ldfe9gSHP.eJw.png", category: "Chapter 4: Tissue Level", options: ["A", "B", "C", "D"] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-57", questionText: "Which of the following figures shows tissue whose functions are secretion? "hyperplasia", "metaplasia"] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-69", questionText: "Discuss how a damaged tissue is repaired.", answerText: "In order to replace the worn-out, damaged or dead cells, new cells arise from stroma, supporting connective tissue, or the parenchyma for replacement. In some cases stem cells, which are the immature and undifferentiated cells, will divide to replace the lost or damaged cells.", category: "Chapter 4: Tissue Level", options: [] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-70", questionText: "Discuss the difference(s) between an endocrine and an exocrine gland.", answerText: "The secretory product (hormones) of endocrine glands enters the interstitial fluid and then diffuses into the blood without going through a duct. Exocrine glands secrete their products into ducts that empty onto the surface of a covering or the lumen of a hollow organ. Sweat, oil, earwax, saliva and digestive enzymes are typical exocrine gland secretions.", category: "Chapter 4: Tissue Level", options: [] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-71", questionText: "A tissue has these characteristic: apical surface, single layer of tall narrow cells, brush border, goblet cells. F", category: "Chapter 4: Tissue Level", options: [] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-73", questionText: "Support electrical excitable cells found in the nervous system are called", answerText: "neuroglial cells", category: "Chapter 4: Tissue Level", options: ["neurons", "neuroglial cells", "fibroblasts", "adipocytes"] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-74", questionText: "Electrical signals called ________ travel along the plasma membrane of a neuron and stimulate the release of chemicals called ________ that are then used to communicate with other cells.", answerText: "action potentials, neurotransmitters", category: "Chapter 4: Tissue Level", options: ["neurotransmitters, action potentials", "action potentials, neurotransmitters", "hormones, enzymes", "ions, proteins"] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-75", questionText: "Discuss the three functional classifications of exocrine glands secretions.", answerText: "The classification is based on how their secretion is released. Merocrine gland secretions are packaged into a secretary vesicle and released via exocytosis. Apocrine glands accumulate their secretory product at the apical surface of the cell and the upper portion of the cell pinches off from the rest of the cell to release the products. Holocrine glands accumulate their product in their cytosol and after the cell matures, ruptures and releases the product.", category: "Chapter 4: Tissue Level", options: [] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-76", questionText: "Anne suffers from Ehlers-Danlos, a rare inherited disorder of connective tissue, specifically affecting collagen fibers. a) Dense irregular connective tissue
b) Elastic connective tissue
c) Dense regular connective tissue
d) Areolar connective tissue
e) Blood connective tissue", answerText: "A, D, E", category: "Chapter 4: Tissue Level", options: ["A, B, C", "A, D, E", "B, C, D", "C, D, E"] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-77", questionText: "Anne suffers from Ehlers-Danlos, a rare inherited disorder of connective tissue, specifically affecting collagen fibers. Because many connective tissues contain collagen throughout the body, she has issues with many tissues, joints, and skin. Name 4 tissues that would be affected and where in the body would the damage be seen.", answerText: "Areolar connective tissue in the papillary region of the dermis and the subcutaneous region of the body. Reticular connective tissue's reticular fibers would be affected due to them being modified collagen. The stroma would not be as supportive. Dense regular in ligaments and tendons. Dermis, sclera and the periosteum and perichondrium's dense irregular would be affected. Elastic connective tissue located in lungs and blood vessels. Fibrocartilage located in the meniscus and intervertebral discs. Bone would lose much of its strength.", category: "Chapter 4: Tissue Level", options: [] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-78", questionText: "An infant has a genetic disorder that affects the thermogenic gene in brown fat. What may be an outcome of this disorder?", answerText: "Difficulty regulating body temperature", category: "Chapter 4: Tissue Level", options: ["Obesity", "Difficulty regulating body temperature", "Dehydration", "Muscle weakness"] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-79", questionText: "What connective tissue is replaced by bone in the epiphyseal plates?", answerText: "Hyaline cartilage", category: "Chapter 4: Tissue Level", options: ["Fibrocartilage", "Elastic cartilage", "Hyaline cartilage", "Dense irregular connective tissue"] }, { id: "q-ch4-qz-80", questionText: "Match the membrane with the appropriate description:
